sources.coverage_note: G20 financial regulation commitments: OTC derivatives reform, shadow banking, too-big-to-fail, compensation, data gaps, and emerging topics.

provisions.title: OTC Derivatives: Central Clearing
provisions.text: All standardised OTC derivative contracts should be traded on exchanges or electronic trading platforms, where appropriate, and cleared through central counterparties by end-2012 at the latest. OTC derivative contracts should be reported to trade repositories. Non-centrally cleared contracts should be subject to higher capital requirements. This commitment was made at the Pittsburgh Summit in September 2009 and represents a cornerstone of the G20 financial reform agenda.

provisions.title: OTC Derivatives: Trade Reporting
provisions.text: All OTC derivative contracts should be reported to trade repositories. Regulators should have access to trade repository data to identify risks. Trade reporting requirements should apply to all asset classes including interest rate, foreign exchange, credit, equity, and commodity derivatives.

provisions.title: OTC Derivatives: Margin Requirements
provisions.text: Non-centrally cleared OTC derivative contracts should be subject to mandatory margin requirements. Both initial margin and variation margin should be exchanged between counterparties. The margin framework is intended to reduce systemic risk by ensuring that adequate collateral is available to cover counterparty credit risk exposures arising from non-centrally cleared derivatives.

provisions.title: Too-Big-To-Fail: Enhanced Regulation of SIFIs
provisions.text: Systemically important financial institutions (SIFIs) should be subject to a higher degree of regulation and more intensive supervision. G-SIBs should maintain higher loss-absorbing capacity, be subject to more intensive supervisory oversight, have recovery and resolution plans, and be subject to resolvability assessments. The FSB should develop a framework for identifying G-SIFIs and for the additional requirements to which they should be subject.

provisions.title: Compensation Reform
provisions.text: G20 leaders endorsed the FSB Principles for Sound Compensation Practices and their Implementation Standards. Financial institutions should: align compensation with prudent risk-taking; ensure boards actively oversee compensation systems; make compensation arrangements transparent; and limit variable compensation as a percentage of total net revenue when it is inconsistent with maintaining a sound capital base. Supervisors should assess compensation practices as part of their supervisory process.

provisions.title: Shadow Banking: Strengthening Oversight and Regulation
provisions.text: G20 leaders committed to strengthening the regulation and oversight of shadow banking to address risks from entities and activities outside the regular banking system. The FSB was tasked with developing recommendations for: banks' interactions with shadow banking entities; money market funds; other shadow banking entities; securitisation; and securities lending and repos. The objective is to ensure that shadow banking is subject to appropriate oversight and regulation.

provisions.title: Resolution Regimes: FSB Key Attributes
provisions.text: G20 leaders endorsed the FSB Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes. All jurisdictions should have in place resolution regimes with the tools and powers to resolve financial institutions in an orderly manner without taxpayer exposure to loss. G-SIFIs should have recovery and resolution plans. Cross-border cooperation agreements should be established for all G-SIFIs. Resolvability assessments should be conducted regularly.

provisions.title: TLAC: Adequate Loss-Absorbing Capacity for G-SIBs
provisions.text: G20 leaders endorsed the FSB proposal for a common international standard on total loss-absorbing capacity (TLAC) for G-SIBs. The TLAC standard ensures that G-SIBs have sufficient loss-absorbing and recapitalisation capacity so that critical functions can be maintained during and after resolution without requiring recourse to public funds.

provisions.title: Correspondent Banking: Maintaining Access
provisions.text: G20 leaders expressed concern about the decline in correspondent banking relationships and the potential impact on financial inclusion and the ability of jurisdictions to send and receive international payments. The G20 called on the FSB, FATF, and other relevant bodies to take action to clarify regulatory expectations on CDD for correspondent banking, support domestic capacity in jurisdictions at risk of losing correspondent banking access, and strengthen information sharing between home and host supervisors.

provisions.title: Completing the Basel III Reforms
provisions.text: G20 leaders endorsed the finalisation of the Basel III reforms (often referred to as Basel IV). The finalised package includes the revised standardised approaches for credit risk and operational risk, the output floor, the leverage ratio framework, and the revised market risk framework (FRTB). The reforms are to be implemented by 1 January 2023, with the output floor phased in through 1 January 2028.

provisions.title: Crypto-Asset Regulation
provisions.text: G20 leaders called for the effective regulation and supervision of crypto-asset activities and markets and committed to implementing the FSB's high-level recommendations for the regulation, supervision and oversight of crypto-asset activities and markets, and of global stablecoin arrangements. Regulation should follow the principle of 'same activity, same risk, same regulation' to ensure a level playing field between crypto-asset activities and traditional financial activities.

provisions.title: Climate-Related Financial Risks
provisions.text: G20 leaders supported the FSB roadmap for addressing climate-related financial risks and endorsed the work of the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) in developing a global baseline of sustainability disclosure standards. Leaders called for consistent and comparable climate-related financial disclosures and supported the implementation of the ISSB standards.

provisions.title: Cross-Border Payments: Enhancing Speed and Cost
provisions.text: G20 leaders endorsed the FSB roadmap for enhancing cross-border payments. The roadmap establishes quantitative targets to be achieved by 2027: cost (no more than 1% for remittances with global average below 3%); speed (75% of payments credited within one hour); access (all end users should be able to send/receive at least one type of cross-border payment); and transparency (all payment service providers should provide a minimum level of information on cost, speed, and tracking). The roadmap covers five focus areas: payment system interoperability, legal/regulatory/supervisory frameworks, data exchange, AML/CFT procedures, and existing payment infrastructures.

provisions.title: Data Gaps Initiative (DGI-3)
provisions.text: The G20 Data Gaps Initiative, now in its third phase (DGI-3, launched 2022), aims to address data gaps exposed by the global financial crisis and subsequent developments. DGI-3 covers 14 recommendations in three categories: climate change data (including physical and transition risk indicators, emissions data for the financial sector, and sustainable finance statistics); distributional information (household, non-financial corporate, and government sector wealth and income distribution); and fintech and financial inclusion data (digital money, cross-border fintech activities).

provisions.title: NBFI Resilience: Post-COVID Reforms
provisions.text: Following the March 2020 market turmoil, G20 leaders tasked the FSB with developing a comprehensive programme to address NBFI vulnerabilities. Key reform areas include: money market fund reforms (reducing structural vulnerabilities that lead to run risk); open-ended fund liquidity management (ensuring fund redemption terms align with underlying asset liquidity); margining practices (reducing procyclicality in margin requirements during stress); and bond market liquidity (improving resilience of core government and corporate bond markets). Implementation is monitored by the FSB through annual progress reports.

provisions.title: AI in Finance: Governance and Regulation
provisions.text: G20 leaders have recognised the opportunities and risks arising from the use of artificial intelligence in the financial sector. Key areas of focus include: model risk management for AI/ML models (explainability, validation, governance); data governance and privacy; third-party dependencies (concentration in AI service providers); consumer protection; and operational resilience. The G20 supports the work of the FSB and standard-setting bodies in assessing the financial stability implications of AI and developing appropriate regulatory approaches.

provisions.title: Tokenisation of Finance
provisions.text: G20 leaders have asked the FSB to monitor developments in the tokenisation of financial assets and assess the potential implications for financial stability. Key considerations include: the impact on market structure and liquidity; legal certainty for tokenised assets; interoperability between tokenised and traditional financial systems; custody and settlement arrangements for tokenised assets; and cross-border regulatory coordination. The FSB has published analysis on the financial stability implications of tokenisation, noting both potential benefits (efficiency, reduced settlement times) and risks (operational, liquidity, and legal risks).

definitions.definition: The perception that the failure of certain large, complex, and interconnected financial institutions would cause severe disruption to the financial system and economic activity, leading to the expectation that governments would intervene to prevent their failure.

Credit intermediation that takes place partly or fully outside the regular banking system. Also known as shadow banking. Includes money market funds, hedge funds, finance companies, securitisation vehicles, and other entities.

national_mappings.requirement_summary: UK implements CDD requirements through the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017.
US implements CDD through the FinCEN CDD Rule requiring covered financial institutions to identify and verify beneficial owners of legal entity customers.
Germany implements CDD through the Money Laundering Act (Geldwaeschegesetz), which transposes the EU Anti-Money Laundering Directives.

national_mappings.national_reference: EU Regulation 575/2013 as applied via Finansinspektionen
national_mappings.requirement_summary: Sweden applies Basel III capital requirements through the EU CRR/CRD framework as supervised by Finansinspektionen.

national_mappings.national_framework: Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds
national_mappings.requirement_summary: Japan requires risk-based approaches to AML/CFT under the APTCP and supervisory guidance from JFSA.
national_mappings.gap_notes: FATF 2021 mutual evaluation identified areas for improvement in risk-based supervision of certain non-financial businesses.

national_mappings.requirement_summary: US implements Basel III capital requirements through Federal Reserve Regulation Q and OCC regulations for national banks. Implementation has been tailored with US-specific modifications.
national_mappings.gap_notes: US Basel III 'endgame' final rule implementation delayed; original Basel IV timeline not met.

national_mappings.requirement_summary: UK implements the FSB Key Attributes through the Banking Act 2009 (as amended by the Financial Services Act 2012 and Bank Recovery and Resolution Order 2014). The Bank of England is the resolution authority.
